section.title: Solomon Made King
section.sectionRationale: 1 Kings opens exactly where 2 Samuel left off: David is old, failing, and no longer able to keep himself warm, let alone govern with a firm hand. Into that vacuum steps Adonijah, David's oldest surviving son, who exalts himself as king without his father's knowledge or the LORD's appointment. Nathan the prophet and Bathsheba move quickly, reminding David of his sworn oath that Solomon would reign, and the aged king rises one final time to act, having Solomon anointed and enthroned at Gihon while Adonijah's own feast is still underway. The chapter closes with Adonijah's coup collapsing in terror and Solomon extending him unexpected mercy.
section.thesis: While David's failing strength opens the door to a son's self-appointed grab for the throne, the LORD's covenant promise prevails as Nathan and Bathsheba move the king to act, and David has Solomon anointed and enthroned according to the oath he swore by the LORD, so that the true king is established not by human ambition but by God's faithfulness to His word.
section.frameNote: Five beats: David old and Adonijah exalting himself, verses 1 to 10; Nathan and Bathsheba appealing to the king, verses 11 to 27; David renewing his oath to Bathsheba, verses 28 to 31; Solomon anointed king at Gihon, verses 32 to 40; and Adonijah's fear turning to mercy, verses 41 to 53.

verses.text: Now King David was old and well along in years, and though they covered him with blankets, he could not keep warm.
So his servants said to him, “Let us search for a young virgin for our lord the king, to attend to him and care for him and lie by his side to keep him warm.”
Then they searched throughout Israel for a beautiful girl, and they found Abishag the Shunammite and brought her to the king.
The girl was unsurpassed in beauty; she cared for the king and served him, but he had no relations with her.
At that time Adonijah, David’s son by Haggith, began to exalt himself, saying, “I will be king!” And he acquired chariots and horsemen and fifty men to run ahead of him.
(His father had never once reprimanded him by saying, “Why do you act this way?” Adonijah was also very handsome, born next after Absalom.)
So Adonijah conferred with Joab son of Zeruiah and with Abiathar the priest, who supported him.
But Zadok the priest, Benaiah son of Jehoiada, Nathan the prophet, Shimei, Rei, and David’s mighty men would not join Adonijah.

And Adonijah sacrificed sheep, oxen, and fattened calves near the stone of Zoheleth, which is next to En-rogel. He invited all his royal brothers and all the men of Judah who were servants of the king.
But he did not invite Nathan the prophet, Benaiah, the mighty men, or his brother Solomon.

units.label: Nathan and Bathsheba appeal to the king

verses.text: Then Nathan said to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon, “Have you not heard that Adonijah son of Haggith has become king, and our lord David does not know it?
Now please, come and let me advise you. Save your own life and the life of your son Solomon.

Go at once to King David and say, ‘My lord the king, did you not swear to your maidservant, “Surely your son Solomon will reign after me, and he will sit on my {{kisse|throne}}”? Why then has Adonijah become king?’
Then, while you are still there speaking with the king, I will come in after you and confirm your words.”
So Bathsheba went to see the king in his bedroom. Since the king was very old, Abishag the Shunammite was serving him.
And Bathsheba bowed down in homage to the king, who asked, “What is your desire?”
“My lord,” she replied, “you yourself swore to your maidservant by the LORD your God: ‘Surely your son Solomon will reign after me, and he will sit on my throne.’
But now, behold, Adonijah has become king, and you, my lord the king, do not know it.

And he has sacrificed an abundance of oxen, fattened calves, and sheep, and has invited all the other sons of the king, as well as Abiathar the priest and Joab the commander of the army. But he has not invited your servant Solomon.
And as for you, my lord the king, the eyes of all Israel are upon you to tell them who will sit on the throne of my lord the king after him.
Otherwise, when my lord the king rests with his fathers, I and my son Solomon will be counted as criminals.”
And just then, while Bathsheba was still speaking with the king, Nathan the prophet arrived.
So the king was told, “Nathan the prophet is here.” And Nathan went in and bowed facedown before the king.
“My lord the king,” said Nathan, “did you say, ‘Adonijah will reign after me, and he will sit on my throne’?

For today he has gone down and sacrificed an abundance of oxen, fattened calves, and sheep, and has invited all the sons of the king, the commanders of the army, and Abiathar the priest. And behold, they are eating and drinking before him, saying, ‘Long live King Adonijah!’
But me your servant he has not invited, nor Zadok the priest, nor Benaiah son of Jehoiada, nor your servant Solomon.
Has my lord the king let this happen without informing your servant who should sit on the throne after my lord the king?”

units.label: David renews his oath to Bathsheba
units.note: as surely as the LORD lives

verses.text: Then King David said, “Call in Bathsheba for me.” So she came into the king’s presence and stood before him.
And the king swore an oath, saying, “As surely as the LORD {{chai|lives}}, who has redeemed my life from all distress,
I will carry out this very day exactly what I swore to you by the LORD, the God of Israel: Surely your son Solomon will reign after me, and he will sit on my {{kisse|throne}} in my place.”
Bathsheba bowed facedown in homage to the king and said, “May my lord King David live forever!”
Then King David said, “Call in for me Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada.” So they came before the king.
“Take my servants with you,” said the king. “Set my son Solomon on my own mule and take him down to Gihon.
There Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet are to {{mashach|anoint}} him king over Israel. You are to blow the ram’s horn and declare, ‘Long live King Solomon!’
Then you shall go up with him, and he is to come and sit on my throne and reign in my place. For I have appointed him ruler over Israel and Judah.”
“Amen,” replied Benaiah son of Jehoiada. “May the LORD, the God of my lord the king, so declare it.
Just as the LORD was with my lord the king, so may He be with Solomon and make his throne even greater than that of my lord King David.”
Then Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada, along with the Cherethites and Pelethites, went down and set Solomon on King David’s mule, and they escorted him to Gihon.
Zadok the priest took the horn of oil from the tabernacle and {{mashach|anointed}} Solomon. Then they blew the ram’s horn, and all the people proclaimed, “Long live King Solomon!”
All the people followed him, playing flutes and rejoicing with such a great joy that the earth was split by the sound.
Now Adonijah and all his guests were finishing their feast when they heard the sound of the ram’s horn. “Why is the city in such a loud uproar?” asked Joab.
As he was speaking, suddenly Jonathan the son of Abiathar the priest arrived. “Come in,” said Adonijah, “for you are a man of valor. You must be bringing good news.”
“Not at all,” Jonathan replied. “Our lord King David has made Solomon king.
And with Solomon, the king has sent Zadok the priest, Nathan the prophet, and Benaiah son of Jehoiada, along with the Cherethites and Pelethites, and they have set him on the king’s mule.
Zadok the priest and Nathan the prophet have anointed him king at Gihon, and they have gone up from there with rejoicing that rings out in the city. That is the noise you hear.
Moreover, Solomon has taken his seat on the royal {{kisse|throne}}.

The king’s servants have also gone to congratulate our lord King David, saying, ‘May your God make the {{shem|name}} of Solomon more famous than your own name, and may He make his throne greater than your throne.’ And the king has bowed in worship on his bed,
saying, ‘Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel! Today He has provided one to sit on my throne, and my eyes have seen it.’”
At this, all the guests of Adonijah arose in terror and scattered.
But Adonijah, in fear of Solomon, got up and went to take hold of the horns of the altar.
It was reported to Solomon: “Behold, Adonijah fears King Solomon, and he has taken hold of the horns of the altar, saying, ‘Let King Solomon first swear to me not to put his servant to the sword.’”
And Solomon replied, “If he is a man of character, not a single hair of his will fall to the ground. But if evil is found in him, he will die.”
So King Solomon summoned Adonijah down from the altar, and he came and bowed down before King Solomon, who said to him, “Go to your home.”

kisse.gloss: Throne, the seat of royal authority. The chapter turns on who will occupy David's kisse: Adonijah seizes it presumptuously while it is not his to take, while Solomon receives it by the LORD's appointment and his father's sworn oath. The Davidic throne was promised an heir forever, 2 Samuel 7:16, a promise finally and fully kept in Christ, the true and eternal King, Luke 1:32-33.

mashach.gloss: To anoint, to consecrate by the pouring of oil, marking someone set apart to an office such as king or priest. Zadok anoints Solomon at Gihon exactly as David commanded, a public, decisive act that ends Adonijah's self-appointed bid for the throne. This word underlies mashiach, Anointed One, a title borne fully by Jesus Christ, the great Son of David.

shem.gloss: Name, and by extension reputation, honor, or renown. The king's servants pray that God would make Solomon's shem more famous than David's own, a blessing spoken over the new king that anticipates the name above every name given to Christ, Philippians 2:9-10.

chai.gloss: Living, alive. David swears by the LORD who chai, lives, and who has redeemed his life from every distress, grounding his oath and Solomon's succession in the character of the living God rather than in human scheming or court politics.

basic.context: David is now old and frail, no longer able even to keep warm, and into that weakness Adonijah, his oldest surviving son, exalts himself as king without his father's knowledge or the LORD's appointment. Nathan the prophet and Bathsheba act quickly, reminding David of his sworn oath that Solomon would reign, and the old king rises to act one last time, having Solomon publicly anointed and enthroned at Gihon. Adonijah's feast collapses in terror when the news arrives, and he flees to the altar in fear, where Solomon shows him surprising mercy. The key questions are why Adonijah felt free to seize the throne, what finally moves David to act, and what it means that God's promise prevails not through strength but through faithful, timely obedience.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recounting a real royal succession, not a manual for how thrones should transfer today. What it teaches as an enduring truth is that the LORD keeps His covenant word even when human ambition and a leader's weakness threaten to derail it, and that legitimate authority rests on God's appointment and calling rather than self-assertion.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is threaded together by the kisse, the throne, repeated again and again as the prize both grasped for and rightly given. Zadok is told to mashach, anoint, Solomon, the same root behind mashiach, Anointed One. And when the servants pray that Solomon's shem, name, would exceed David's own, the chapter's vocabulary quietly points toward a King whose name would one day be above every name.
basic.typology: Solomon is anointed and enthroned by his father's command while David still lives, seated on David's own throne in fulfillment of a sworn promise, a pattern that anticipates Christ, installed as King by the Father's own decree. Adonijah's presumptuous self-exaltation warns against every false claim to glory not given, while Solomon's mercy toward his defeated rival previews the gospel's welcome to rebels who come humbly seeking refuge.
basic.christ: Solomon does not seize the throne; it is given him by his father's oath and the LORD's own appointment, sealed by anointing before witnesses. So the Son does not grasp at equality with God but is given all authority by the Father, and is seated at His right hand, Acts 2:30-33. And where Solomon shows mercy to a rebel who comes seeking refuge at the altar, Christ shows infinitely greater mercy to sinners who come to Him in humble faith.
basic.god: God is faithful to His covenant word even when the human means of keeping it look fragile, an old, weakening king and a prophet and a mother forced to intervene. He works through ordinary courage and timely speech rather than spectacle, and He is merciful even toward those who set themselves against His chosen king, giving Adonijah a genuine opportunity to prove himself rather than striking him down at once.
basic.crossReferences: 2 Samuel 7:12-16 records the LORD's covenant promise of an enduring house and throne for David's offspring. 1 Chronicles 22:9-10 shows David already knew, before this chapter, that Solomon was the LORD's chosen heir. Psalm 72 is a prayer for the righteous reign of just such a king. And Luke 1:32-33 declares that Jesus will be given the throne of His father David forever.
basic.oneStory: The Davidic covenant promised an heir who would reign forever, and here that promise passes its first great test, a rival's coup, a father's weakness, and a kingdom hanging in the balance. Solomon's anointing secures the line for another generation, and points ahead through the whole history of Israel's kings to Jesus, the true and final Son of David, whose throne cannot be seized, only received from the Father.

context.literary: The chapter opens with the pathetic image of David unable to warm himself, immediately establishing the vacuum of leadership that Adonijah rushes to fill. The narrative alternates scenes with careful pacing: Adonijah's feast outside the city, Nathan and Bathsheba's urgent appeals inside David's chamber, the public anointing at Gihon, and finally the report that reaches Adonijah's feast just as it winds down, ending his celebration in terror.
context.historical: David's failing health at the end of a forty-year reign created real uncertainty, since no clear law of primogeniture governed Israel's kingship and the LORD's choice, not birth order, determined the heir. Gihon was a spring just outside Jerusalem, a natural site for public ceremony, while En-rogel, where Adonijah feasted, lay further off and out of the city's immediate hearing, until the noise of celebration carried the news. Grasping the horns of the altar was a recognized ancient Near Eastern plea for asylum and mercy.
context.canonical: This chapter begins 1 and 2 Kings, the historical record of Israel's monarchy from its height under Solomon to its division and eventual exile. It answers directly to 2 Samuel 7's covenant promise of an eternal Davidic throne, and it sets the pattern the rest of the book will follow, kings measured by their faithfulness to the LORD, with Solomon's early promise here soon to be complicated by the compromises of chapters to come.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD's covenant promise of a Davidic heir prevails over human ambition and a king's physical weakness, secured through the faithful, timely action of Nathan, Bathsheba, and David, and to present Solomon's kingship as legitimately established by anointing and oath rather than self-assertion.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific ancient succession crisis and its resolution; it does not prescribe a method of royal succession for every era, nor does it prescribe court intrigue as a model for pursuing God's will. What endures is the pattern that God keeps His covenant word, that legitimate authority comes from His appointment, and that self-exaltation apart from His calling is presumption rather than faith.

debates.question: Do Nathan and Bathsheba's coordinated appeal to the ailing David amount to manipulation of a feeble old man, or wise, faithful action?

views.label: Faithful, necessary action to secure God's revealed will
views.case: David had already sworn by the LORD that Solomon would reign, and 1 Chronicles 22:9-10 shows David knew before this chapter that the LORD Himself had chosen Solomon; Nathan and Bathsheba are not inventing a claim but reminding the king of a settled, sworn promise that Adonijah's coup was actively overturning. Their urgency matches the urgency of the crisis, since Adonijah had already been proclaimed king by a large company while David remained uninformed.

views.label: A calculated court intervention that reads more like political maneuvering
views.case: The oath to Bathsheba is reported only by Nathan and Bathsheba themselves, with no earlier record of David swearing it in the text as we have it, and the elaborate two-stage plan, Bathsheba speaking first and Nathan corroborating, has the shape of court strategy as much as simple reminder.

debates.bearing: Either way, the narrative itself treats the oath as genuine and David accepts it without hesitation or protest, and the outcome is presented as the LORD's will being accomplished rather than palace intrigue triumphing over it; the chapter commends decisive, timely faithfulness to what God has already promised.

debates.question: How should readers assess David's fitness and culpability in this chapter, given both his physical incapacity and his past failure to correct Adonijah?

views.label: David's age and infirmity are simply the occasion, not a moral failure
views.case: Verses 1 to 4 present David's frailty sympathetically, and as soon as he is informed of Adonijah's coup he acts swiftly, decisively, and righteously in verses 28 to 53, proving that his kingship, though physically weakened, remained spiritually sound and capable of faithful leadership when roused to act.

views.label: Verse 6 functions as a real, if gentle, criticism of David's earlier parenting
views.case: The narrator's aside, 'his father had never once reprimanded him,' echoes the same parental passivity that marked David's earlier failures with Amnon and Absalom, suggesting Adonijah's presumption grew, in part, from David's own long pattern of not confronting his sons.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that even Israel's greatest king was flawed and finite, unable on his own to guarantee a righteous succession, which is precisely why the chapter roots Solomon's kingship in the LORD's sworn word rather than in David's unaided strength, and why the true King Israel needed would have to be greater than David himself.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's vocabulary circles around the kisse, the throne, named again and again as the prize Adonijah grasps for and the gift Solomon receives. Zadok and Nathan are commanded to mashach, anoint, Solomon at Gihon, the very root behind mashiach, the Anointed One. And when David's servants pray that God would make Solomon's shem, name, more famous than David's own, the chapter's own language already gestures toward a coming King whose name and throne would exceed anything David's dynasty had yet seen.

typology.type: Solomon anointed and enthroned at his father's command while David still lives
typology.fulfillment: Christ, installed as King by the Father's own decree and seated at His right hand, Psalm 2:6-7, Acts 2:30-33.

typology.type: Adonijah's self-exaltation apart from the LORD's calling
typology.fulfillment: Every false claim to glory not given contrasted with Christ, who did not exalt Himself but was appointed by the One who said to Him, You are My Son, Hebrews 5:5.

typology.type: Solomon's unexpected mercy toward Adonijah at the altar
typology.fulfillment: The mercy of the true King, who does not deal with rebels according to what their sin deserves, but grants refuge to those who come humbly before Him, Romans 5:8-10.

study.christ: Solomon does not seize the throne for himself; it is sworn to him by his father and confirmed by anointing before witnesses at Gihon. In the same way, the Son does not grasp at glory but receives all authority from the Father, given the name above every name and seated at His right hand, Philippians 2:9-11, Acts 2:33. Where Adonijah's presumption ends in terror at the altar, Solomon's mercy toward him, on the condition that he prove himself faithful, foreshadows the far greater mercy Christ extends to every rebel who comes to Him for refuge, not on the basis of merit, but of grace received in humility.
study.god: God is faithful to keep His covenant word even when the human means look fragile: an old, failing king, a prophet who must act boldly, and a mother pleading for her son's life. He is not dependent on strong or unblemished agents to accomplish His purposes, but works through David's last obedient act, Nathan's courage, and Bathsheba's appeal. And He is merciful even in judgment, allowing Adonijah, who set himself against the LORD's anointed, a genuine chance to live rather than striking him down immediately.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's covenant promise that an offspring of David would have an established house, kingdom, and throne forever.
David had already been told by the LORD that Solomon, a man of peace, would build the house for His Name and sit on the throne.
A parallel account of Solomon being anointed and enthroned, with the record that all Israel obeyed him.
A prayer for the king's son to judge with righteousness and defend the afflicted, the standard by which every Davidic king, including Solomon, is measured.
The angel's announcement that Jesus will be given the throne of His father David and will reign forever, the promise this chapter serves.

study.oneStory: The LORD had promised David an heir whose throne would be established forever, and here that promise meets its first real test: a rival's coup, an aging king's weakness, and a kingdom hanging in the balance. Through the faithful action of Nathan and Bathsheba and David's final obedient command, Solomon is anointed and enthroned exactly as sworn, carrying the covenant line forward one more generation. The whole scene points ahead through the long, uneven history of Israel's kings to Jesus, the true and final Son of David, whose throne is not seized by ambition but freely given by the Father, and who will reign forever.

quiz.q: Why does Adonijah declare himself king while David is still alive?

options.text: He exalts himself presumptuously, gathering chariots and supporters on his own initiative, without the LORD's appointment or David's knowledge, v5-10.
options.note: This is exactly how the narrative introduces him, acting on his own initiative apart from God's appointment.

options.text: As the oldest surviving son, born next after Absalom, he assumed the throne was naturally his to take.
options.note: True background detail, v6, but the text frames his action as self-exaltation, not lawful inheritance by birth order.

options.text: David had already promised Adonijah the throne before his death.
options.note: False; David had sworn the opposite to Bathsheba concerning Solomon, verses 13, 17, and 30.

options.text: Zadok, Nathan, and Benaiah anointed Adonijah as the rightful king.
options.note: False; these very men explicitly refused to join Adonijah, verse 8.

quiz.q: What finally moves David to act decisively about the succession?

options.text: Nathan and Bathsheba's appeal reminding him of his sworn oath by the LORD that Solomon would reign after him, v13, 17, 29-30.
options.note: This is precisely the appeal that leads directly to David's oath and command.

options.text: Reports that Adonijah had begun raising chariots and horsemen for himself.
options.note: This is real background, verse 5, but it is not what directly prompts David's response in this scene; the prophetic and maternal appeal is.

options.text: Joab's warning that the army was turning against David.
options.note: Not in the text; Joab in fact supports Adonijah rather than warning David, verse 7.

options.text: David decided the matter entirely on his own initiative with no prompting from anyone.
options.note: False; Nathan and Bathsheba specifically initiate and carry out the appeal, verses 11-27.

quiz.q: What does Zadok and Nathan's anointing of Solomon at Gihon accomplish?

options.text: It publicly and decisively establishes Solomon as king according to David's command, ending any real doubt about who will reign, v32-40.
options.note: The anointing is public, ceremonial, and immediately effective, confirmed by the people's proclamation and rejoicing.

options.text: It formally begins Solomon's reign, though the outcome remains genuinely uncertain until Adonijah's guests disperse.
options.note: True that the news still has to reach Adonijah's feast, but the anointing itself is already decisive and public, not tentative.

options.text: It is a private, symbolic ceremony known only to the priests involved.
options.note: False; it is thoroughly public, with trumpet, procession, and the people's rejoicing so loud the earth seems split, verse 40.

options.text: It has no legal or religious significance for Solomon's kingship.
options.note: False; anointing by the priest and prophet at the king's command is precisely what confers legitimate kingship in this scene.

quiz.q: How does Adonijah respond when he learns Solomon has been made king, and how does Solomon treat him?

options.text: Adonijah flees in fear to grasp the horns of the altar, and Solomon shows him mercy on the condition that he prove himself a man of character, v50-53.
options.note: This matches the text precisely, including the explicit condition Solomon attaches to his mercy.

options.text: Adonijah immediately submits and receives an unconditional pardon with no terms attached.
options.note: Close, but Solomon's mercy does carry an explicit condition, that evil not be found in him, verse 52.

options.text: Adonijah gathers his remaining supporters to fight Solomon for the throne.
options.note: False; his guests scatter in terror and Adonijah seeks refuge rather than fighting, verses 49-50.

options.text: Solomon has Adonijah put to death immediately upon hearing of the coup.
options.note: False; Solomon spares him at this point in the story, verses 52-53.

quiz.q: What does this chapter reveal about how God's promises are kept amid human ambition and scheming?

options.text: Even though David is frail and Adonijah acts in self-serving haste, the LORD's covenant word to give Solomon the throne prevails through the faithful, timely actions of Nathan, Bathsheba, and David.
options.note: This is the theological center of the chapter, God's promise kept through ordinary, faithful obedience rather than spectacle.

options.text: God's promises are kept only because David personally overpowers Adonijah's forces in battle.
options.note: There is no battle in this chapter; the promise is secured through anointing and legitimate authority, not military conquest, so this overstates a conflict that never happens.

options.text: The chapter shows that human schemes like Adonijah's are usually the ones that succeed over God's revealed will.
options.note: This is contrary to the outcome; Adonijah's scheme collapses swiftly once David acts.

options.text: The chapter shows God is uninvolved and the outcome is decided purely by political maneuvering.
options.note: False; the narrative repeatedly frames the outcome as fulfillment of David's oath sworn by the LORD, not mere politics.

questions: Adonijah exalted himself as king, assuming his position and charm entitled him to the throne. Where are you tempted to grasp at something God has not given you rather than waiting on Him?
David's failing strength opened the door to a crisis. What areas of weakness in your own life are you tempted to hide rather than bring honestly before God and trusted others?
Nathan and Bathsheba acted quickly and courageously to remind David of his sworn word. Is there a faithful, perhaps uncomfortable, word you need to speak into someone else's life right now?

Solomon's throne was secured not by his own strength but by his father's oath and the LORD's appointment. Where do you need to rest in what God has promised rather than striving to secure your own future?
Solomon showed Adonijah mercy rather than immediate judgment. Who in your life might need you to extend patience and mercy rather than the response their actions may seem to deserve?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes 'the stone of Zoheleth' may alternatively be rendered 'the Serpent's Stone', a landmark near En-rogel just outside Jerusalem.
The BSB footnote observes that the Hebrew reads simply 'the commanders,' while the Septuagint specifies 'Joab the commander,' naming him directly among those feasting with Adonijah.
The BSB footnote notes the Septuagint renders David's words as 'one of my offspring' rather than simply 'one,' making explicit that it is his own son who now sits on the throne.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 1:1-53. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
